metadata.name: Shaurya Gupta
metadata.title: Independent Researcher
metadata.tagline: I study what happens to human judgment when decisions route through machines.

researchQuestions.title: What happens when institutions delegate judgment to AI systems?
researchQuestions.subtitle: The central organizing question
researchQuestions.description: AI makes decision premises — forecasts, classifications, recommendations — cheap to produce. The institutional work of authorizing them (review, contestation, accountability) does not get cheaper at the same rate. This program studies what happens in that gap: institutions don't collapse, but authority migrates from substantive judgment toward procedural and infrastructural proxies for it.

researchQuestions.title: At what point does human oversight saturate, and can you detect it from the outside?
researchQuestions.subtitle: Formal modeling + observable telemetry
researchQuestions.description: Modeled as a two-queue system: AI production throughput against fixed human authorization capacity. Above a threshold, institutions substitute fast ceremonial sign-off for slow substantive review. The open question this program keeps returning to: can that substitution be detected from ordinary operational telemetry, without ever inspecting the content of the decisions themselves?

researchQuestions.title: Do people anchor on machine-proposed numbers the way they anchor on human ones?
researchQuestions.subtitle: Behavioral asymmetry in human-AI delegation
researchQuestions.description: A controlled multi-phase battery, a failed cross-model replication, a public-data meta-analysis, and a pending human-subjects study — used to test whether the classic anchoring effect behaves the same way when the anchor comes from a machine.

researchQuestions.title: Can we trust the proxies used to measure institutional and regulatory behavior?
researchQuestions.subtitle: Measurement validity as its own research question
researchQuestions.description: SEC comment-letter counts, corporate-delisting price benchmarks, governance-document accountability language, ML evaluation metrics on small tabular data — four unrelated-looking literatures share the same failure mode: the proxy is treated as the thing it measures. This question asks, case by case, whether that substitution holds up against the regulator's own records.

researchQuestions.title: Does AI change labor-market institutions the same way it changes governance ones?
researchQuestions.subtitle: Applied ML as object of institutional study
researchQuestions.description: An attrition-prediction model is not just a model — it's a decision an HR department will authorize or rubber-stamp. This question treats a standard applied-ML pipeline as a case study in the same authorization dynamics the theoretical work describes, and audits it accordingly: calibration, explainability stability, and fairness, not just accuracy.

researchQuestions.title: Why do national AI governance strategies diverge instead of converging?
researchQuestions.subtitle: Comparative governance framing
researchQuestions.description: Nine national AI strategies, coded on four frames. Everyone claims competitiveness. What splits along regime type is the second-order justification — rights and ethics in liberal democracies, sovereignty and security in centralized developmental states. The divergence looks technical; it is closer to a legitimation contest.

papers.title: Bounded Coordination: Organizational Attention, Accountability, and the Limits of AI Absorption
papers.abstract: Institutions historically organized around scarce cognition. AI reverses the scarcity: forecasts, classifications, and recommendations become cheap to generate, and the organization's real constraint becomes its capacity to legitimately absorb them. Develops "bounded coordination" as the successor construct to Simon's bounded rationality, built from three mechanisms — coordination bandwidth, epistemic opacity friction, and decision augmentation layers — and traces the resulting failure modes: automation bias, procedural gridlock, displaced accountability.
papers.keyFinding: Organizations don't fail to absorb AI outputs from a lack of cognition — they fail from a lack of legitimate authorization capacity, a formally distinct bottleneck.

papers.title: Adjudicative Capacity: Institutions, Expertise, and the Legitimacy of AI-Generated Knowledge
papers.abstract: Institutions do not simply receive AI-generated claims — they adjudicate them, converting interpretive material into authoritative knowledge through procedure, professional judgment, and infrastructure. Argues the pressure AI places on institutions is adjudicative, not evidential: institutions rarely collapse outright under machine-generated content, but authority quietly migrates from substantive judgment to procedural and infrastructural proxies for it — a process this paper calls legitimacy migration.
papers.keyFinding: The scarce resource under AI conditions is not information but the institutional capacity to certify it — and that capacity migrates, rather than vanishes, once exceeded.

papers.title: Performing Algorithmic Responsibility: Ceremonial Governance and Institutional Legitimacy in AI Adoption
papers.abstract: AI governance artifacts — ethics boards, audit committees, monitoring dashboards — are frequently ceremonial: real in that they exist and consume resources, but decoupled from substantive technical control, especially where the underlying infrastructure is vendor-controlled. Develops four mechanisms of ceremonial governance — promissory enrollment, assurance surfaces, jurisdictional buffering, dependency normalization — that let organizations perform responsibility without exercising it.
papers.keyFinding: The presence of an AI governance structure is not evidence it is doing governance work — sometimes it is evidence of the opposite.

papers.title: Modeling Authorization Saturation: A Coupled Queueing Model of Behavioral Tipping Points in AI-Governed Institutions
papers.abstract: Formalizes the authorization-production asymmetry as a coupled two-queue system: an AI recommendation-production queue and a human authorization queue with state-dependent service rates. Above a closed-form utilization threshold ρ* = k/(1+k), the model predicts a behavioral tipping point at which institutions substitute fast, low-quality ceremonial sign-off for slow, substantive review. Four governance interventions — capacity investment, throughput caps, quality floors, and a hybrid — are tested by discrete-event simulation.
papers.keyFinding: Doubling reviewer capacity or halving AI throughput restores review quality to baseline; raising the quality floor of ceremonial review alone reduces damage but does not eliminate the transition.

papers.title: Capacity Degradation in Distributed Human-in-the-Loop AI Pipelines: A Queueing-Network Analysis of Oversight Saturation
papers.venue: ADCAIJ — Advances in Distributed Computing and Artificial Intelligence Journal, Universidad de Salamanca, Vol. 15 (2026)
otherVenues: earlier drafts adapted for an AAAI AI Governance workshop, a NeurIPS workshop, and MIT's Undergraduate Research Technology Conference
papers.abstract: Introduces the State-Estimation Degradation Index (SEDI), an oversight-quality metric computable purely from boundary telemetry — timestamps, queue depth, dispute rates — without access to the content of the decisions under review. Validated first on a non-circular agent-based synthetic pipeline, then on 22 months of real GitHub pull-request telemetry across four public repositories (scikit-learn, pandas, transformers, langchain), where a 33× load surge on langchain produced a detectable saturation signature as review latency collapsed from 91 hours to 8.5.
papers.keyFinding: Oversight saturation leaves a detectable signature in ordinary operational telemetry, months before it would show up in any content-based audit.

papers.title: Comment Letters Are Not Reviews: Validating a Common Proxy for SEC Oversight Against the Regulator's Own Statistics
papers.venue: S.T. Yau Science Award, Economic & Financial Modeling track
papers.abstract: A body of empirical work proxies SEC regulatory review intensity using EDGAR comment-letter correspondence. Checks that proxy against the SEC's own published statistics and finds it fails on two counts: the "active filer" denominator used in prior work rose 186% over a period in which the SEC's own reporting-issuer count fell 20% — reversing the direction of the headline trend when recomputed correctly — and the comment-letter channel captures only 40–62% of actual SEC reviews, with roughly a quarter of reviews carrying undocumented oral comments.
papers.keyFinding: A widely used regulatory-oversight proxy reverses sign once checked against the regulator's own denominator.

papers.title: Who Owns the Decision? Human Oversight and Responsibility in AI Governance Texts
papers.abstract: Codes AI-governance documents — vendor terms, university policy, public-sector directives, regulatory guidance — on eight binding-authority dimensions (accountable actor, decision authority, review duty, override power, documentation, appeal, audit, error ownership) to measure how specifically responsibility is actually assigned, versus merely invoked. Current pilot corpus: 23 documents, mean Accountability Specificity Index (ASI) 7.5 of 16.
papers.keyFinding: Governance texts frequently invoke oversight rhetorically without binding it to a named actor, a documentation duty, or an appeal channel — a pattern this paper calls accountability laundering.

papers.title: Simulating AI Governance Failure: A Computational Model of Procedural Capacity Thresholds
otherVenues: adapted for an IMF student research conference
papers.abstract: A Monte Carlo model of procedural capacity — an institution's joint ability to review, contest, and hold someone accountable for an AI-influenced decision — loosely calibrated against the Dutch child-benefits scandal (Toeslagenaffaire) as a real case where all three capacities failed together. Simulates how review capacity, contestability, and accountability routing interact as automation load increases.
papers.keyFinding: The three components of procedural capacity fail in a specific, non-arbitrary order as automation load crosses institution-specific thresholds — which intervention helps depends on which stage of failure you're already in.

papers.title: Evaluation Inflation in Constrained Tabular Classification: Taxonomy, Quantification, and a Reproducible Correction Protocol
papers.abstract: Grew out of debugging a fairness-audit pipeline. On small, class-imbalanced tabular problems, standard reporting practice systematically overstates model quality through six distinct mechanisms — discrimination overconfidence, calibration neglect, attribution overreach, cluster-label reification, fairness-conclusion inflation, and split-stability assumption. Names and quantifies each, then proposes a reproducible correction protocol.
papers.keyFinding: Several of these six inflation mechanisms were present in the author's own earlier attrition-prediction manuscript before revision — this paper is partly a public accounting of that experience, generalized into a checkable taxonomy.

papers.title: A Governance-Aware Framework for Auditing Calibration, Explainability, and Fairness in HR Attrition Prediction Models
papers.abstract: A nine-stage audit pipeline for employee-attrition prediction models, covering calibration (expected calibration error), explainability stability (bootstrap SHAP), and fairness (demographic parity, equalized odds, proxy mutual information) on the IBM HR Analytics dataset. Following major revision, every figure and reported statistic is now generated end-to-end from a single reproducible pipeline (5-fold CV CatBoost, AUC 0.810 ± 0.019, calibration error 0.043).
papers.keyFinding: Global calibration hides substantial zone-level miscalibration in the model's least-confident predictions — the part of the model an HR reviewer would actually need to scrutinize most closely.

papers.title: Human-AI Anchoring Asymmetry
papers.abstract: Do people anchor on a machine-proposed number the way they anchor on a human-proposed one? An 18,000-call audited language-model battery found AI-generated anchors produce contrast rather than the assimilation effect textbook anchoring predicts. Reconciling that against a ten-study meta-analysis of LLM anchoring research and against the public Many Labs 1 human-anchoring dataset (n = 6,344) suggests the effect is anchor-type dependent: informative, embedded anchors assimilate; incidental, comparative anchors under uncertainty contrast. Own human-subjects data collection (target n = 120) is pending institutional review board approval.
papers.keyFinding: The anchoring asymmetry between humans and AI is not fixed — it depends on anchor type, reconciling an apparently contradictory prior literature rather than adding one more contradictory data point to it.

papers.title: Benchmark Observability in Corporate Delisting: Route Choice, Disclosure Design, and the Provenance of Financial Training Data
papers.abstract: Audits 58 Indian corporate delisting transactions for which pricing-benchmark type survives in the public record and finds a roughly 19:1 asymmetry: statutory floor prices persist far more often than market-referenced comparables — a direct consequence of which delisting route a firm chooses under SEBI's disclosure rules, not random data loss. Any valuation or compliance system, including an AI one, trained on this archive inherits that regulatory-design-driven gap as strategic missingness.
papers.keyFinding: A regulatory disclosure regime can silently determine which financial benchmarks are even available to train or audit a model against, long before anyone touches machine learning.

papers.title: Governing by Justification: Regime Legitimation and the Framing of National AI Strategies
papers.abstract: Codes nine national AI strategies — Australia, Canada, China, France, India, Saudi Arabia, Singapore, UAE, UK — across four frames: competitiveness, sovereignty-security, ethics-rights-inclusion, and international cooperation. Competitiveness is dominant or co-dominant in every case, but the second-order frame splits cleanly along regime type: liberal democracies foreground ethics and rights, centralized developmental states foreground sovereignty and security.
papers.keyFinding: AI-governance fragmentation across countries looks like a disagreement about technology, but the frame split tracks regime-legitimation strategy more closely than it tracks any technical disagreement.

methods.name: Formal / Queueing-Theoretic Modeling
methods.description: Closed-form and simulated queueing models of institutional capacity — coupled production/authorization queues, state-dependent service rates, saturation thresholds.

methods.name: Behavioral Experiments
methods.description: Controlled estimation tasks with human and language-model participants, testing anchoring and judgment under different agent-source framings.

methods.name: Human-AI Comparison
methods.description: Comparing behavior when the same estimation task is anchored by a human-attributed versus machine-attributed number.

methods.name: Machine Learning / Calibration & Fairness Auditing
methods.description: Predictive modeling with reproducibility, calibration, explainability-stability, and fairness auditing as first-class outputs, not afterthoughts.

methods.name: Text-as-Data / Structured Content Coding
methods.description: Manual and AI-assisted coding of governance and policy text into structured variables, with explicit inter-rater reliability reporting.

methods.name: Regulatory-Archive & Proxy Validation Analysis
methods.description: Checking a commonly used research proxy — comment-letter counts, delisting price benchmarks — against the regulator's or market's own primary records.

methods.name: Monte Carlo / Case-Calibrated Simulation
methods.description: Stochastic simulation of institutional processes, loosely calibrated against a documented real-world failure case rather than fit to proprietary data.

experiments.title: Phase 21: Full Main Battery (Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite)
experiments.finding: AI-generated anchors produce contrast, not the assimilation textbook anchoring predicts (condition C0).

experiments.title: Phase 22: Cross-Model Replication
experiments.finding: Total failure, not partial: gemini-2.5-pro output was consumed entirely by hidden reasoning tokens under a misconfigured token budget; gemini-2.0-flash was inaccessible on the available API key. Zero usable cross-model evidence — root-caused and the approach abandoned.

experiments.title: Meta-Analysis of LLM Anchoring (k = 10 studies)
experiments.finding: Supports an anchor-type moderator: embedded/informative anchors assimilate across the literature; the incidental, uncertainty-framed anchors used in this program's own data contrast.

experiments.title: Many Labs 1 Public Human-Anchoring Replication
experiments.finding: Humans assimilate on all 4 anchor conditions tested (AI-index 0.22 / 0.50 / 0.69 / 0.59), the external-validity leg the program needed before collecting new human data.

experiments.title: Own Human-Subjects Data Collection
experiments.finding: Design and instruments are built and self-tested; recruitment cannot begin until institutional review board approval clears.

experiments.title: Discrete-Event Simulation of Authorization Saturation
experiments.finding: Confirms the analytical threshold ρ* = k/(1+k) in the finite-server case and quantifies which governance interventions actually restore review quality.

experiments.title: Real GitHub PR Telemetry Validation (SEDI)
experiments.finding: langchain's 33× load surge produces a detectable hybrid-SEDI saturation signature as review latency collapses from 91 hours to 8.5 — the first real-world (non-synthetic) positive result for the metric.

experiments.title: AIES Pilot Corpus Expansion
experiments.finding: Corpus and coding instrument hold up as they scale, but an early draft's overclaimed 80-document corpus and a described-as-completed practitioner experiment were both cut before submission once neither existed yet.

datasets.name: Anchoring Study Data (Phase 21, condition C0)
datasets.description: Locked, audited 18,000-call language-model anchoring battery — the primary evidentiary asset for the anchoring-asymmetry program.

datasets.name: SEC Comment Letter Corpus (EDGAR)
datasets.description: Regulatory filing correspondence used to test the comment-letter-as-oversight-proxy claim against the SEC's own published statistics.

datasets.name: Many Labs 1 Public Human-Anchoring Data
datasets.description: Public replication dataset (OSF) used as the external-validity leg of the anchoring-asymmetry program — not collected by this project, cited as secondary evidence.

datasets.name: GitHub PR Telemetry (Oversight Saturation)
datasets.description: 22 months of real pull-request telemetry across scikit-learn, pandas, transformers, and langchain, pulled via the GitHub search API to validate SEDI on real-world data.

datasets.name: AI Governance Text Corpus (ASI)
datasets.description: Vendor terms, university policy, public-sector directives, and regulatory guidance, hand-coded on 8 accountability dimensions for the Accountability Specificity Index.

datasets.name: Indian Corporate Delisting Transaction Records
datasets.description: 58 audited transactions (pricing-benchmark layer) and a 2,239-event administrative layer of Indian delistings under SEBI's 2009 and 2021 regulations.

datasets.name: IBM HR Analytics Attrition Dataset
datasets.description: Public, simulated employee-attrition dataset used for the calibration/fairness/explainability audit and its follow-on evaluation-inflation methods paper.

datasets.name: Open Institutional Capacity Observatory — Public Institutional Datasets
datasets.description: Small, real, public-source pilot tables (EOIR, USCIS, CFPB, SEC) plus the ASI-adjudicated matrix, released as shared infrastructure so this program (and others) stop rebuilding the same scraping and provenance pipelines from scratch.
datasets.license: CC BY 4.0 (OICO-derived tables); source records retain original government/public terms

failures.title: The regulatory-debt thesis died on contact with real SEC data
failures.whatWeThought: SEC oversight of financial disclosures, proxied by EDGAR comment-letter volume, was declining relative to the number of active filers — a growing 'regulatory debt.'
failures.whatHappened: Recomputing the active-filer denominator against the SEC's own published reporting-issuer statistics reversed the trend: the paper's denominator had risen 186% over a period the SEC's own count fell 20%. The comment-letter channel itself also turned out to capture only 40–62% of actual reviews.
failures.whatWeDid: Did not bury the result. Rewrote the paper around the falsification itself: a methods paper validating (and rejecting) a proxy commonly used across the literature, checked against the regulator's own numbers.

failures.title: The attrition-audit figures were drawn from illustrative constants, not the model
failures.whatWeThought: The submitted HR-attrition audit's figures reflected the actual output of the nine-stage calibration/fairness pipeline.
failures.whatHappened: Discover AI's reviewers found the earlier figure-generation script used summary values embedded directly in the plotting code rather than pipeline outputs — a traceability failure, not a modeling one, but one that made every reported number unverifiable.
failures.whatWeDid: Rebuilt a single end-to-end pipeline that computes and emits every statistic used downstream; the figure script now reads only from those artifacts. Numbers changed slightly (AUC 0.810 ± 0.019, not the earlier figure); the pipeline is now fully reproducible from raw data.

failures.title: Cross-model replication was a total loss, not a partial one
failures.whatWeThought: Replicating the anchoring-asymmetry finding on a second and third language model would be a straightforward robustness check.
failures.whatHappened: One model's entire output budget was consumed by hidden reasoning tokens under a misconfigured token limit (2,250/2,250 calls unusable); the other model was inaccessible on the available API key. Zero cross-model evidence resulted — worse than a null finding, since no comparison was possible at all.
failures.whatWeDid: Root-caused the failure, then redesigned the remaining program around zero API dependency: a public meta-analysis of existing LLM-anchoring studies plus a public human-anchoring dataset, reserving the one available API key for the original locked battery.

failures.title: The oversight metric was validated against a data-generating process it shared assumptions with
failures.whatWeThought: SEDI's correlation with review depth in the synthetic validation pipeline was evidence the metric identifies genuine oversight degradation.
failures.whatHappened: A pre-submission self-audit found the synthetic data-generating process encoded the same depth-to-latency and depth-to-dispute relationships the metric was built to recover — a circularity that would not survive review.
failures.whatWeDid: Built a non-circular agent-based synthetic pipeline instead, then added hybrid baselining (rolling baselines for efficiency-confounded signals, fixed baselines for disputes) to resolve a related efficiency/degradation confound — detection AUC rose from 0.62 to 1.00 on the corrected design.

failures.title: An early draft claimed a corpus and an experiment that didn't exist yet
failures.whatWeThought: The accountability-specificity paper was ready to describe an 80-document corpus and a completed practitioner sign-off experiment.
failures.whatHappened: Neither existed: the real corpus at that point was a 23-document pilot, and the practitioner experiment was a design, not a result.
failures.whatWeDid: Cut both claims before the manuscript went anywhere near a reviewer, and reframed the paper honestly around what had actually been coded and validated so far.

failures.title: Wrong genre for the target journal
failures.whatWeThought: An econometric framing of the delisting-benchmark findings would translate cleanly into a submission for a historical-essay journal.
failures.whatHappened: A self-run editorial-fit simulation against that journal's actual standard scored the econometric draft near zero — right findings, wrong genre entirely.
failures.whatWeDid: Rewrote the paper from scratch in the target genre (archival case narrative with primary-source citation, not regression tables) rather than forcing the wrong format into the wrong venue.

failures.title: Two government data sources didn't survive the retrieval audit
failures.whatWeThought: SSA and Meta institutional-capacity snapshots could round out the observatory's first public data release.
failures.whatHappened: Both were flagged during data archaeology as invalid or too weakly retrieved to trust.
failures.whatWeDid: Excluded them from the v1 release rather than ship five sources where one might be wrong. Quarantined with a report explaining why, for anyone who wants to try recovering them properly later.

notebookEntries.title: First 23 governance documents, coded by hand
notebookEntries.body: Hand-coded the first batch of the accountability-specificity pilot — vendor terms, one university AI policy, a couple of regulatory guidance documents. The 8-dimension instrument holds up on a small sample. Most documents score in the middle: they name someone, but not an appeal channel or a documentation duty.

notebookEntries.title: Caught myself overclaiming before anyone else had to
notebookEntries.body: Drafted a paragraph describing an 80-document corpus and a completed practitioner sign-off experiment. Neither exists yet — the real corpus is 23 documents, hand-coded, and the experiment is a design on paper. Cut both. Slower is fine; wrong is not.

notebookEntries.title: The regulatory-debt thesis is dead
notebookEntries.body: Recomputed the active-filer denominator against the SEC's own reporting-issuer statistics. The trend reverses. Real result, wrong direction. This is now a proxy-validation paper, not a regulatory-debt paper — a cleaner and more useful contribution than the one I set out to write.

notebookEntries.title: Self-audited the oversight metric before submission
notebookEntries.body: Ran a hostile audit on the SEDI paper before anyone else could. Found the synthetic validation pipeline shared its generating assumptions with the metric it was supposed to validate — circular. Rebuilding the synthetic design and adding hybrid baselining before this goes anywhere near a reviewer.

notebookEntries.title: Cross-model replication: total loss
notebookEntries.body: Phase 22 is a total failure, not a partial one — 2,250 calls to one model burned entirely on hidden reasoning tokens under a token-budget misconfiguration, 2,250 to another on an inaccessible endpoint. Zero cross-model evidence. Redesigning the rest of the program around zero API dependency instead of chasing a fix with one API key.

notebookEntries.title: First real-world signal for SEDI
notebookEntries.body: Pulled 22 months of real pull-request telemetry from four public repositories. langchain's load surge — 33× over eight months — shows up in the hybrid SEDI signal as review latency collapses from 91 hours to 8.5. First positive result on real data, not synthetic.

notebookEntries.title: Observatory released — cut two data sources that didn't hold up
notebookEntries.body: Released the Open Institutional Capacity Observatory at v1.0.0-rc1: five metrics (QAI, SEDI, Authorization Saturation, Procedural Capacity, ASI), public datasets, benchmarks, replication slots for four of this program's own papers. Excluded SSA and Meta snapshots — they didn't survive the retrieval audit. Four clean sources beats five where one is questionable.

notebookEntries.title: Major revision — and the reviewer was right
notebookEntries.body: Discover AI sent back a major revision on the attrition audit. Reviewer 3 found it: the figures were drawn from illustrative constants in the plotting code, not the model's actual output. Uncomfortable to read. Correct.

notebookEntries.title: Rebuilt the pipeline end to end
notebookEntries.body: New pipeline computes every reported number from the raw data, in one run: CV AUC 0.810 ± 0.019, calibration error 0.043. The headline finding also changed — global calibration was hiding much worse calibration in the model's least-confident quartile. Slower pipeline, uglier code, correct numbers.

notebookEntries.title: Many Labs gives the anchoring program its external-validity leg
notebookEntries.body: Public Many Labs 1 human-anchoring data (n = 6,344) shows assimilation on all four conditions — humans behave the way the textbook predicts. That's the missing comparison point: the asymmetry isn't AI being weird, it's AI behaving differently from a well-established human baseline. Own human data collection is still blocked on IRB approval.

guestbook.name: Research Collaborator
guestbook.message: Rare combination of theoretical rigor and shipping speed — he builds the instrument, then writes the paper.

guestbook.name: Methods Reviewer
guestbook.message: Asks the uncomfortable methodological questions first. The proxy-validation harness changed how our group codes text data.

guestbook.name: Platform User
guestbook.message: The observatory is the first academic infrastructure I've used that feels like it was built to be reused, not just published once.

timeline.title: Started with applied ML audits
timeline.description: Began with a standard applied-ML pipeline — attrition prediction — treated as a case study rather than a demo: calibration, explainability stability, and fairness as first-class outputs.

timeline.title: A reviewer catches a reproducibility failure
timeline.description: Discover AI's major-revision decision on the attrition audit forces a full pipeline rebuild. The uncomfortable finding — figures traced to illustrative constants, not model output — becomes the seed of a separate methods paper on evaluation inflation.

timeline.title: The institutional-theory thread opens
timeline.description: Reading into Simon, Meyer & Rowan, and Luhmann while debugging the ML pipeline suggests a bigger question: is 'review' behaving the same way at the institutional level as at the model level? Bounded Coordination and Adjudicative Capacity begin as parallel theoretical papers.

timeline.title: Formalizing oversight saturation
timeline.description: The theory becomes a formal queueing model (SBP-BRiMS) and an observable metric (SEDI, for ADCAIJ). A pre-submission self-audit catches a circularity in the SEDI validation design before it reaches a reviewer; the fix — hybrid baselining — becomes the paper's actual contribution.

timeline.title: A regulatory thesis falls apart, honestly
timeline.description: A parallel measurement-validity thread — checking whether SEC comment letters really proxy for regulatory review — reverses under the regulator's own data. Rewritten as a proxy-validation methods paper instead of buried.

timeline.title: Building shared infrastructure
timeline.description: The individual metrics (SEDI, Authorization Saturation, Procedural Capacity, ASI, QAI) get consolidated into the Open Institutional Capacity Observatory — public data, reproducible benchmarks, and reserved replication slots for each paper in the program.

timeline.title: Closing the loop with real human data
timeline.description: The anchoring-asymmetry program is the one thread still waiting on the hardest kind of evidence: original human-subjects data. Design, instruments, and analysis code are built and self-tested; recruitment is blocked on institutional review board approval.
